data.title: Bloom Energy's AI Monetization Is Here - Upside Potential Mostly Priced-In
data.description:
I previously covered Vistra Corp. (VST) in August 2025 and Rolls-Royce Holdings (OTCPK:RYCEY) (OTCPK:RYCEF) in August 2024, discussing their robust nuclear power prospects at a time of multi-year data center capex boom.

In this article, I shall discuss Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) and share my findings about the stock, continuing the theme surrounding the power generation sector.

BE Is A Beneficiary Of The Ongoing AI Boom

For the uninitiated, BE is a company offering stationary fuel cell energy platform across the generation of electricity through the Bloom Energy Server® and the production of hydrogen through the Bloom Electrolyzer™.

For now, the US is facing an aging utility infrastructure, attributed to the fact that "much of the infrastructure was built 50-75 years ago and is struggling to keep up with modern energy demands, weather events, and the transition to renewable energy," worsened by the "46% of distribution infrastructure (is) beyond its useful life.”

With the first commercial SMR projects only expected "to start operation around 2030" and the global data center power demand expected to grow drastically from 415 TWh in 2024 to 1,700 TWh by 2035 (based on Lift-Off Case), expanding at a CAGR of +13.6%, it is unsurprising that some hyperscalers and data center REITs have opted to utilize BE's fuel cell offerings.

This is because its fuel cells are able to deliver reliable, scalable, and clean onsite power that can be rapidly deployed without legacy grids, allowing them to mitigate the prolonged wait of "up to 2 years for grid power access."

These reasons are also why some hyperscalers and data center REITs are already pursuing onsite power generation to supplement utility grids, with BE expecting "in 2030, 38% of facilities are expected to use some onsite generation for primary power, up from 13% a year ago. Notably, 27% of facilities expect to be fully powered by onsite generation by 2030, a 27x increase from just 1% last year."

These developments may have directly contributed to BE's outsized FQ3'25 performance across:

  • the robust total revenues of $519M (+29.3% QoQ/+57% YoY),
  • the richer adj gross margins of 30.4% (+5.1 points YoY/+14.2 from FQ4'19 levels of 16.2%),
  • the expanding adj EBITDA margins of 11.3% (+4.9 points YoY/+9.6 from FQ4'19 levels of 1.7%), and
  • the adj EPS of $0.15 (+50% QoQ/+1600% YoY),

with it exemplifying its ability to capitalize on the ongoing data center boom and the power constrained environment.

BE 1Y Stock Price

These developments may have contributed to the bulls buoying the BE stock to new heights never seen before, with the outsized 1Y rally well outperforming the wider market and its fuel cell peers by large degrees (before the recent AI-related market-wide meltdown).

This is especially since the fuel cell company may deliver another beat and raise performance in the FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in early 2026, as observed in the YTD total revenue outperformance at $1.24B (+37.7% YoY) and hinted by the management in the recent earnings call:

Turning to the full year. As K.R. mentioned, based on what we see today, we expect fiscal 2025 to be better than our previously stated annual guidance on our financial metrics. (Seeking Alpha)

This development is not overly speculative as well, as BE monetizes the available inventory sum of $704.99M in FQ3'25 (+2.1% QoQ/+20.6% YoY) and delivers on the multi-year product backlog of $2.5B reported in the July 2025 Investor Presentation along with the multi-year service backlog of $9.6B (+5.4% YoY) in the FY2024 earnings call.

At the same time, the management has highlighted the ongoing capacity expansion "to 2 gigawatts by December 2026, which will support about 4x our 2025 revenue" ... "as we enter into 2027."

These numbers imply an extremely bullish FY2027 total revenue opportunity of $7B (likely well spread across product, installation, service, and electricity), based on the last offered FY2025 total revenue guidance of $1.75B at the midpoint (+19% YoY) while suggesting an accelerated 3Y CAGR of +68.2% from FY2024 total revenues of $1.47B.

BE's FY2027 total revenue opportunity does not appear overly aggressive indeed, thanks to the highly strategic partnerships with numerous players, including:

  • a $5B partnership with Brookfield Asset Management (BAM:CA), for the deployment of BE's fuel cell technology in future AI factories,
  • a 1 GW supply agreement with American Electric Power (AEP),
  • a partnership with Equinix (EQIX), a pure-play data center REIT, with 100MW of fuel cell capacity already deployed,
  • a micro-grid solution with Quanta Computer (OTC:QUCCF), and
  • numerous fuel cell deployment at Oracle's (ORCL) data centers in the US.

The expanded capacity appears necessary as well, as observed in the robust demand for its fuel cell offerings through the 400 MW deployed on a YTD basis as of July 24, 2025 compared to the current annual capacity of 1GW.

As a result of the growing sales and the nascency of its bottom-line reversal, I am of the opinion that BE may also deliver richer adj gross margins and adj EPS performance moving forward as it eventually benefits from the improved manufacturing and operational scale, respectively.

FOMO Feeding BE's Prior Upward Momentum

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why the consensus forward estimates appear to be somewhat conservative, with BE's expanding manufacturing capacity, growing partnerships, and higher fuel cell deployments seemingly discounted.

While I am not so ambitious as to suggest a 100% capacity utilization in 2027 to warrant the aggressive FY2027 total revenue estimates of $7B, the consensus current FY2027 total revenue estimates of $4.1B imply a relatively low utilization rate of ~41%.

This is based on the LTM product revenue ratio of 71.6% against total revenues, with further upsides more likely than not, based on the recent performance metrics and delivery cadence.

BE Valuations

Otherwise, there is no denying that BE remains rather expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 13.59x, FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 113.09x, and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 193.20x, compared to the sector median of 2.10x, 11.79x, and 19.86x, respectively, despite the recent stock price correction by -29% from the 52 week heights.

When comparing BE's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 13.59x and the consensus (conservative) 3Y total revenue growth estimates at a CAGR of +41.1% to its fuel cell/power generation peers, including:

  • FuelCell Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FCEL, OTCPK:FCELB) at 2.36x/+26.7%,
  • Plug Power Inc. (NASDAQ: PLUG) at 5.47x/+18.6%, and
  • Ballard Power Systems Inc. (BLDP), (BLDP:CA) at 3.81x/+7%, respectively,

it is apparent that the market's exuberance surrounding BE's AI-related power generation tailwinds has been overly done, as FOMO investors/traders feed its upward momentum.

Even if I am to assume an extremely bull-case FY2027 total revenue of $7B, I am still looking at a somewhat inflated estimated FY2027 EV/Sales valuations of 3.68x, based on the Enterprise Value of $25.77B at the time of writing - with it implying a uncertain return profile for those whom buy in at current levels.

BE's Outsized Rally Overly Done - Initiate Hold

BE 7Y Stock Price

Based on the price chart above, BE has had an excellent 2025 outperformance, as the bulls defend its nearly vertical upward momentum since July 2025. Well, this is before the popping of the AI bubble by early November 2025 and the subsequent meltdown across the AI supply chain.

BE Technical Indicators

Despite so, BE's technical indicators remain somewhat overstretched at current levels, as observed in the inflated trading volumes beyond historical levels, with it remaining to be seen when the bulls may eventually defend a floor worsened by the elevated short interest of 15.46% and the ongoing profit taking.

Barring a full utilization of its growing capacity and the acceleration in its growth profile in the higher double digits to match the premium valuations, I am of the opinion that BE presents a relatively risky investment thesis at current levels.

While I congratulate those whom have gotten in at the 2024/2025 lows, I do not recommend anyone to buy this dip, attributed to the potential capital losses at current nosebleed levels.

I am initiating a Hold (Neutral) rating on the BE stock here.

data.title: Fidelity Short-Term Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Short-Term Bond Fund is a diversified, short-term bond strategy that seeks a high level of current income, consistent with preservation of capital.
  • Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Year Government/Credit Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance that is commensurate with investor expectations of a high-quality short-term bond fund.
  • Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including asset allocation, sector and security selection, yield-curve positioning and opportunistic trading.
  • Robust governance and risk management, consisting of extensive quantitative modeling, formal and informal portfolio reviews, and proprietary tools, support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.03% in the third quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Over the three-month period, investor sentiment shifted from caution to relative optimism, as concerns about U.S. trade and fiscal policy cooled and fixed-income investors welcomed the resumption of policy interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Year to date, the Aggregate index has gained a robust 6.13%.

Following a strong June – when the index rose 1.54% – the third quarter got off to a sluggish start, with the bond market taking a small step back in July (-0.26%). Bond yields moved higher and credit spreads widened after data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics showed inflation accelerating in June. Headline inflation rose 2.7% year over year, up from 2.4% in May. The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury, which began the quarter at 4.24%, hit a peak of 4.50% on July 15 before easing to 4.37% by month end. At its July 29-30 meeting, the Fed held rates steady for the fifth consecutive time, maintaining the pause on monetary easing that began in January.

On August 1, a new jobs report showed a sharp slowdown in hiring, with the U.S. economy adding just 73,000 jobs in July. Additionally, job gains for May and June were revised downward. Mid-month, the inflation rate held steady at 2.7%. Meanwhile, corporate earnings remained strong, and the Trump administration's newly implemented tariffs on U.S. trade partners proved less severe and disruptive than the market had initially feared. Taken together, these developments were seen as giving the Fed clearance to resume cutting the federal funds rate at its next meeting in September. The index advanced 1.20% in August.

On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark policy rate by a quarter point (25 basis points), asserting its view that supporting a weakening labor market outweighed concerns about inflation running slightly above target. Bond yields declined and credit spreads tightened in anticipation of this decision, boosting price performance and helping the Aggregate Bond index gain 1.09% for the month. At quarter end, the market expected the Fed to implement additional quarter-point cuts at its two remaining meetings in 2025 before adopting a slower pace in 2026.

Over the quarter, longer-dated bonds with maturities of 10+ years posted the strongest advance at 3.26%, while intermediate and short-term issues lagged, with 7-10 year bonds gaining 2.58%, 5-7 year maturities up 1.82%, and 1-3 year issues rising 1.20%. By credit rating, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Results at the sector level reflected a positive tone for risk assets, with yield-advantaged, credit-sensitive sectors broadly outperforming Treasurys (+1.51%). U.S. corporate bonds (+2.60%), buoyed by a strong earnings season, led among investment-grade categories, followed by government-agency mortgage-backed securities (+2.43%) and government-related bonds (+2.23%). Commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.75%) and asset-backed securities (+1.64%) more narrowly outpaced Treasurys.

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained 2.10%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+2.54%) finished slightly below investment-grade corporates. Notably, emerging-markets debt delivered the bond market's best returns for the quarter – rising 3.58% for investment-grade and 3.09% for high-yield issues.

Performance Review

Detailed Fund Attribution Relative To Benchmark

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment

Amid growing investor appetite for risk, yield-advantaged sectors broadly outperformed U.S. Treasurys. Among investment-grade categories, corporate bonds led the way.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Overall, sector positioning contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Year Government/ Credit Bond Index.

  • Overweighting U.S. corporate bonds aided relative performance. (Positive)
  • A non-benchmark allocation to asset-backed securities, including car loan debt and collateralized loan obligations, also helped. (Positive)
  • In contrast, underweights to U.S. Treasurys and government-related bonds detracted from relative performance. (Negative)

Strategy: Security Selection

Market Environment

By maturity, longer-dated (10+ years) bonds had the strongest gain. By credit quality, securities rated BBB outpaced higher-quality investment-grade bonds.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Security selection among investment-grade corporate bonds added relative value the past three months.

  • Within the industrial sector, picks among consumer non-cyclical corporates contributed. (Positive)

Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve

Market Environment

Yields fell across the maturity spectrum, with declines sharpest at the front end of the curve.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

The fund's yield-curve positioning detracted.

  • The fund was underweight one-year maturities in favor of two-, three- and five-year positions. (Negative)
  • The fund's overall duration was generally in line with that of the benchmark. (Neutral)

Outlook and Positioning

In the third quarter of 2025, the global economic cycle remained in expansion, weathering geopolitical and trade policy uncertainty. The U.S. economic outlook continued to be mixed, with corporate profits and credit conditions improving, while other areas – most notably the labor market – showed signs of softening.

The past three months, fixed-income performance benefited from falling U.S. Treasury yields and tighter credit spreads across all major categories. The yield curve has steepened significantly over the past year – short-term U.S. Treasury rates have declined in line with the trajectory of Fed policy rates, while yields on longer-dated bonds have remained elevated. Ongoing concerns about sticky inflation, rising deficits and Fed independence could keep long-term interest rates relatively high for some time.

As of September 30, about 40% of the fund's assets were invested in corporate bonds. Within corporates, financials remained our largest sector allocation, at roughly 24% of total assets. The portfolio also owned about 35% U.S. Treasurys and a roughly 17% non-benchmark allocation to asset-backed securities, including securitized car loan debt and collateralized loan obligations. Consistent with our strategy, the fund was notably underweight U.S. Treasurys, which represented approximately 72% of the benchmark at quarter end.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market, based on our view of pricing and fundamentals. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management.

It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.

data.title: LyondellBasell: Post Buy Call Analysis - Lessons In Cyclical Timing And Dividend Sustainability
data.description:
In May 2025, I had issued a contrarian Buy Call on LyondellBasell (LYB) banking on comforting cash flow yields and encouraging transition to higher-margin products. Valuations also looked like a dip to initiate contrarian entries. I had noted risks around cyclical demand recovery and execution.

After my Buy call, LYB went up ~18% till July. Dividends were intact in August (improving the total returns). Investors who could capture the gains and book profit at that point, before Q2 earnings were reported, may find another entry today. But for the bulk of long-term investors, the contrarian call has gone dramatically wrong. What has transpired since the call (including the brief rally as expected) has been a sobering net ~26% decline in share prices (~22% including the dividends, in total returns terms).

A fresh look at LYB today reveals systematic issues I erred in identifying in May, and some fresh data that triggers a need to rerate the stock as a Hold. LYB Buy was what shows the classic issues in trying to time the bottom, ignoring the pressures of a cyclical business and a dividend trap setup, that could have been either been avoided, or timed for the short term with defined upside targets for exits (to enable a July high profit booking). Timing a Buy call in May and getting it wrong hurts even more given that the S&P 500 is up by ~12% since then and with markets in a rebound rally from May to October, almost any other Buy would have generated positive returns, as most of my other calls have benefitted from.

Margins, Cash Flows and Dividends

I had argued about a trough in petrochemical margins likely behind us. Gross margins since then do indicate an improvement. Cash generation was strong in Q3. The cash from operating activities was reported as $983m, with a cash conversion rate of 135%, indicating improving product feedstock spreads. In the Olefins & Polyolefins Americas segment, non‑GAAP EBITDA jumped to $428m, a 35% sequential increase, driven by higher utilization (crackers ran at ~95%) and a restart of the Channelview plant. North American integrated polyethylene margins also improved by ~23% QoQ.

Management has also indicated significant ethylene capacity rationalization which could help rebalance supply over time. However, the margin signals are not uniformly green overall. In Europe/Asia/International segments, margin pressure from import competition, especially for polymers, was highlighted. Overall, I feel that while the overall margin trajectory is improving, global margins are still not normalized and improvements are not uniform.

The free cash flow is improving. The TTM free cash flow of $728m is definitely a rebound from the negative YTD numbers till Q3. This has stemmed largely from normalized North American operations and disciplined capex control. A Cash Improvement Plan targets $600m cash flow improvements in 2025 and $1.1b in 2026 based on cost and capex optimization, a defensive move for the uncertain demand trough period. That is an acceptable turn towards cash discipline, even though structural demand-led rebounds would have made a Buy thesis more plausible.

The targeted $1.1b cumulative cash improvement by end-2026 is substantial and does support LYB’s ability to fund dividends compared to the pressures in recent periods of negative free cash flow. If the company executes well against this plan, the incremental cash would provide a meaningful buffer against margin volatility, reduce pressure on working capital and improve the sustainability of dividend payouts.​

However, it's important to note that even with these improvements, actual dividend coverage depends on normalized post-improvement free cash flow levels versus the total dividend obligation. For context, LYB's annual dividend commitment is around $1.7-1.8b based on current payout rates and outstanding shares. The $1.1b cash improvement is relative to baseline projections - it is not additive to current FCF. Even if 2026 free cash flow reaches $1.2-1.3b, the payout coverage still falls short of full sustainability, though the risk diminishes. In short, the dividend sustainability does look better now compared to May, given the cash flow emphasis and improvements, but still remains at the higher end of what the business can comfortably support.​ Any shortfall in margin recovery could pressure the dividend sustainability again.

The latest quarterly dividend of $1.37, declared on November 21, keeps the forward yield at close to 13% of current prices. This shows management's commitment to shareholder returns but also raises dividend sustainability concerns in a volatile operating backdrop. The announcement does remove near-term uncertainty, but genuine durability still rests on LYB delivering the planned cash improvement targets and achieving a steadier margin environment.

​More Expensive Now

At the time of my May thesis, the EV to forward EBITDA was at ~8.5x levels. It is now actually more expensive at ~9.2x, despite the share price corrections. This is understandably due to downgrades in forward EBITDA estimates. The EPS estimates show a 60% contraction in 2025 now. Forward EPS estimates have also been revised downwards, acknowledging the lack of clear visibility of structural turnaround in petrochemical demand. And that is the core reason why I downgrade LYB from a Buy to a Hold despite the corrections and an improving margin and cash flow trajectory. The Buy call in May was based on the expectation of rebound in structural demand. Management buckling down on a defensive cash generation plan based on capex and operational optimization, actually makes for a strong Hold case (addressed dividend sustainability), but also signals the uncertainty in a structural rebound in the near term. When petrochemical demand rationalizes, LYB could become s Buy.

My May thesis is a classic mistake of confusing a cycle trough with inflection. Since then, margins have improved and capex discipline will likely support a better cash flow path now, but signs of inflection in global demand supply dynamics are not uniform.

The transition to higher-margin products also will take time, not an immediate re-rating catalyst. Q3 2025 numbers show the Advanced Polymer Solutions' (APS) $47m contribution to an overall EBITDA of $835m. New plant ramp-up (the chemical-recycling facility, for example) is slated years out and customer qualification cycles remain lengthy. Even if APS margins improve as I had argued earlier, the magnitude of the contribution relative to the core O&P business is modest. This could have been a supportive transition when the larger concerns of a structural rebound was addressed - not a meaningful factor in today's defensive environment.

data.title: Ares Capital Vs. Blue Owl Capital: Which 10%+ Yield Is The Better Buy For Income Investors?
data.description:
If you’re an income investor, it’s been hard to find new things to invest in lately.

With yields on their way back down, bonds have seen distribution yields fall, and with the market at all-time highs, the S&P 500’s dividend yield also looks quite meager.

Even dividend stocks like those found in popular ETFs like SCHD (the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF) have an average yield of only 3.8%, which just isn’t that much.

In order to produce even $50k of annual income, an investor needs more than $1.3 million invested into SCHD, which is out of reach for many.

Despite this, there are still some areas of the market producing solid, well backed, double digit dividend yields. One of those spaces is Business Development Companies, or BDCs for short. If you’ve never heard of BDCs before, they're investment firms that use shareholder capital to originate direct loans to private, middle market businesses. As these loans get paid back, investors receive juicy dividends.

This - and floating rate arrangements - are why many top BDCs are still paying dividend yields of ~10% or higher, even with treasury yields moving south.

In this article, we wanted to take a look at the first and second largest BDCs on the market - Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC)

These companies enjoy strong advantages of scale, considerable diversification, and produce robust, double digit yields.

But which of these two stocks is the better buy for income investors?

Today, we'll dive deep into each firm's prospects and ultimately crown one as the better choice for income-seeking investors.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Dividend Safety

As we mentioned, BDCs are investment companies that use shareholder capital and debt to originate direct loans to private market companies. As a result, the dividends paid by BDCs - and the level of long-term safety - is largely dependent on the portfolio of loans each company chooses to make.

Let’s start by taking a look at ARCC, the largest BDC on the market.

When it comes to consistent long-term dividend payouts, investors should be looking for strong diversification in the portfolio, in addition to a high percentage of loans being well secured by underlying assets.

In the case of ARCC, the loan portfolio is extremely diverse, with 587 total underlying portfolio companies. This means that the largest direct investment ARCC has made only counts for 2% of total outstanding assets:

This is an unbelievable level of diversification across companies.

Similarly, ARCC is well diversified by industry, which means that the firm should do a good job at generating robust loan income through different stages of the market cycle. For example, if energy firms or consumer companies are doing poorly as a result of macro factors, investors are hedged with considerable exposure to other businesses that may be doing better.

Finally, when it comes to ARCC's position on the capital stack, 61% of the firm's loans are 'first lien' senior secured loans, which are the lowest risk type of loans that a BDC can make:

This means that 61% of ARCC's loans are secured by underlying assets, and that ARCC has first crack at trying to liquidate these assets in the event of insolvency or non-payment.

The remaining ~40% of the portfolio is invested into Second Lien senior secured loans, senior direct lending programs, senior subordinated loans and other equity positions, alongside a 7% position with Ivy Hill asset management.

Taken together, more than three quarters of ARCC's total capital is invested into senior secured loans, with varying degrees of asset security. The remainder of ARCC's capital is tied up in equity positions, which should generate additional upside for the company.

When viewed in total, ARCC is a well-diversified company that we expect will be able to support the current dividend well into the future.

Shifting gears now to Blue Owl, the company has a considerably lower number of outstanding borrowers, at only 238, but this is partly a result of the fact that the overall asset base is materially smaller than Ares.

On a per-investment basis, the largest position represents a 3.7% share of OBDC's overall assets, and the top 10 positions represent only 22% of the overall portfolio value. This is slightly more concentrated than Aries on a per company basis, but it’s still within tolerable levels:

Not only that, but OBDC is significantly less concentrated in Internet and software companies, with lower overall concentration than ARCC in any one industry.

As a result, when it comes to portfolio diversification between the two, it’s a bit of a wash. ARCC has more companies and smaller individual positions, but OBDC is considerably less invested into any single sector.

OBDC also has 80% of its outstanding positions as senior secured loans - either as first or second lien - which is a much higher percentage than Ares:

Simply put, we prefer this stance as it protects shareholder equity more aggressively.

Taken together, we expect both BDCs will be able to continue paying dividends well into the future, even if we somewhat prefer Blue Owl's level of overall portfolio diversification.

Dividend Yield

While ARCC and OBDC are somewhat similar from a dividend safety perspective, in our view, there’s a clear winner when it comes to yield.

At present, ARCC yields roughly 9.85%, nearly ~10% per year. This yield is paid out from the companies’s net investment income, and the payout ratio is nearly one-to-one. This means that ARCC pays out basically all of its net income as dividends to shareholders, which is good, but it also means that there’s no room to increase the yield any further.

In the most recent quarter, Aries produced $0.48 per share in net investment income, and the dividend was $0.48 per share:

Blue Owl - on the other hand - enjoys a significantly higher level of dividend yield. The most recent payout announced was $0.37 per share, a rate that we expect the company will be able to pay out continuously, given that the company's two most recent quarters of net investment income were $0.40 and $0.36 respectively.

In this way, OBDC is also paying out the entire net income to investors, but with a cheaper valuation in the stock, it translates to a much stronger forward yield of roughly 13.3%.

As a result, with all things being equal, OBDC is the obvious choice when it comes to pure dividend income.

Finally, when looking at dividend yield on cost, which is a measure of how well a company can sustain and grow payouts over time, ARCC has kept a relatively consistent range between 9% and 13% since inception. This means that the company has done a good job of paying out steady yield to shareholders without bleeding equity value:

To a large degree, OBDC is the same over the last six years, averaging yields of 9% to 13% through most normal market conditions:

As a result, between each firm's overall level of dividend safety and dividend yield, we expect investors in either security to have meaningful double digit yields going forward.

Price

But how is each stock currently priced?

At present, with rates moving lower, it has had a 'subduing' effect on BDCs equity multiples.

Over the last year, ARCC's share price has fallen roughly 10%, while Blue Owl's has fallen more than double that, by roughly 21%:

As we see it, the difference in this selloff is largely what makes up the difference in yield, and in our view, buying OBDC at a stronger discount to equity makes a lot of sense.

Right now, ARCC is trading at a market cap of roughly $14 billion, and the firm's most recent reported book value came in at roughly $14.3 billion. This implies a 3% discount to book, and in a vacuum, every dollar invested into ARCC gets more than a dollar's worth of net assets. Not a bad deal.

With Blue Owl, however, the company trades at a market cap of exactly $6 billion and has a total equity base of $7.6 billion implying and even stronger discount to book value (21%), and an even better 'bang for your buck' per dollar invested.

Recent news around Blue Owl Capital's combination with OBDC II, a non-publicly traded BDC managed by the same company has also contributed to the compressed multiple.

In recent hours, the merger was called off, but for our purposes, we believe a combination would still leave public shareholders with more capital, more diversification, and an increased focus for management.

As a result, we see the recent selloff as an opportunity, not a risk.

Summary

While ARCC remains a well-run, low-risk BDC with a ~10% yield that we believe will be paid well into the future, we prefer OBDC, given it sports a higher overall dividend yield, improved industry diversification, and a stronger discount to book value.

As a result, we're re-iterating our 'Buy' rating on both stocks, with a moderate preference for the latter firm.

Cheers~

data.title: TJX Companies: Solid Quarterly Results, But The Stock Is Expensive
data.description:
The TJX Companies Inc. (TJX) is an off-price apparel and home goods retailer, selling their products worldwide. Despite being a player in the consumer discretionary sector, TJX's stock performed exceptionally this year, outperforming the broader market by more than 7%.

I started covering the firm back in 2022 and maintained my neutral rating ever since, primarily citing valuation as a concern and weak consumer confidence as a tailwind.

The aim of my article today is to assess the latest quarterly results, and give an updated view, whether TJX's stock could be an attractive buy at the current price levels or not.

Quarterly results

Sales

Net sales in the last quarter reached as much as $15.1 billion, representing an increase of 7%, year-over-year. Consolidated comparable sales grew at a slightly lower rate of 5%.

The following table breaks down the net revenue into segments, and we can see that all of TJX divisions managed to grow by high single digits.

TJX managed to grow in each segment. In my view, these results are exceptional. Most firms in the consumer discretionary space are struggling, due to soft demand and poor consumer confidence. And this is exactly the reason why TJX is thriving. When consumer confidence is low, people are less certain about their financial futures and therefore are reluctant to spend large amounts on non-essential, discretionary, durable goods. These consumers either postpone their purchases, or look for cheaper alternatives, and this is where TJX plays a key role.

Even HomeGoods grew by 8%, which is find the most impressive. In my previous articles about retails that sell home goods and furniture, I always argued that the demand for their products is declining not only because of the consumer sentiment, but also because of the weakness in the housing market. But once again, their losses seem to be TJX's gains.

As a result of this strong performance and strong demand, management raised its full year revenue guidance.

In my view, in the current market environment these financial results for a retailer in the consumer discretionary sector is outstanding and could be attractive for investors looking to dip their toes into this sector.

Profitability

TJX did well on the profitability side of things as well. In the third quarter, TJX achieved a gross profit margin of 32.6%, representing a 1 percentage point increase compared to the same period in the prior year. The main reasons for the margin expansion were the higher merchandise margin, due to lower freight costs, and expense leverage.

The firm's pretax profit margin also expanded by 0.4%, reaching 12.7%. Higher SG&A expenses, driven mainly by the incremental store wage and payroll costs partially offset the factors positively impacting the gross margin. Net interest expense also had a 0.1% negative effect on the pretax profit margin.

Looking forward, also important to mention that TJX managed to increase its inventory significantly, pointing out the exceptional buying opportunities they saw in the market place. In my view, this could help TJX have a strong line up of merchandise for the holiday season, potentially leading to strong sales and attractive profitability. When it comes to inventory, however, I also like to point out the risks that come with inventory build up. In case the firm is unable to sell the products, they may need to use extensive discounting to sell them, potentially hurting profitability. But as we see healthy demand for TJX's products across all segments, I do not believe this is likely going to be the case.

Capital allocation

The firm's capital allocation is strongly shareholder focused. The firm returned as much as $1.1 billion to shareholders, out of the $1.5 billion generated in operating cash flows. The company used a combination of dividends as share buybacks to return this amount.

TJX currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.43 per share, equivalent to an annual yield of 1.2%. These amounts seem to be safe and sustainable going forward, especially when considering the strong operational performance. The payout ratios all appear to be healthy and compare favourably to TJX's own historic metrics.

The firm also has a solid history of dividend payments and dividend growth. TJX paid dividends in each of the past 35 years, and also managed to increase them each year consecutively in the past 28 years.

I believe that TJX's shareholder centric capital allocation strategy can be attractive for dividend- and dividend growth investors.

Valuation

No analysis is complete without talking about the share price and the valuation. TJX's share price hit an all-time high in the recent days. So the main question is, if the price ran up already, is there still a buying opportunity?

The following table summarizes a set of traditional price multiples and compares TJX's metrics with the respective sector median, as well as with the firm's own historic metrics.

From the table we can see that TJX is selling at a significant premium compared to the sector median and also compared to its own historic metrics. While the firm's financial performance is excellent and its fundamentals appear solid, I believe that there is little upside from the current price levels.

If I narrow the peer group to firms in the apparel retail industry, we can also see that TJX seems to be the most expensive company.

The premium may be justified, but again, in my view, there is little upside from the current price levels.

Conclusions

TJX posted exceptional quarterly results, with strong sales growth across all segments, margin expansion, and improved guidance.

The firm's capital allocation also remains shareholder focussed, with returning $1.1 billion either in the form of dividends or share buybacks.

In my view the valuation of TJX remains high, offering little upside potential from the current price levels.

For these reasons, I maintain my neutral rating on the firm.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Strong Brand, Stable Margins, But Limited Near-Term Upside
data.description:
The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) has a robust Hold thesis. Margins are improving on product mix and pricing power and so is organic growth. However, there is no immediate catalyst seen for further extraordinary growth on either the topline or margins. And if PG continues to compound growth at current rates, then it ends up justifying its current valuations at best.

I don't see the scope for fresh entries at current levels for a mature but stable business. This despite PG trading near the 52 week lows. Any further correction towards ~$125 is where I would deploy fresh capital. For now, PG could continue to hold strong because of its brand power demanding valuation premiums. Dividends could also remain well supported. Overall, a bland story of income and strong business justifying the "fair" share prices, but an opportunity to lap up more shares.

Growth and Margins

The topline growth is improving after some quarters of stagnation. In the past two quarters, net sales were up ~2% (Q4 FY2025) and ~3% (in Q1 FY2026). The TTM revenue graph seems to be finally moving up due to these two growing quarters pushing trailing revenues above $84b. Drilled down data of the past few quarters show that Beauty and Baby/Feminine categories remain bright spots with above-average organic sales, while Home Care and Grooming are solid, but trends in Health Care and Fabric categories are less robust. In most of the recent quarters, as seen in Q1 FY2026, volumes have been flat. The topline growth has been mostly attributed to product mix, pricing and FX effects. Structurally, that is an endorsement of PG's brand and pricing power, but volume stagnation leads to concerns about continued growth without innovation or new product traction.

Management guidance for FY2026 projects 0-4% organic growth, the midpoint a continuation of current trends. While growth in North America is muted (heightened competitive activity in North America and Europe), China is seeing a reasonable growth trend, particularly with Baby Care, SK-II and Fabric care. Growth is strong in Latin America as well (~7%) and broad based. Traction in premium product mix is also encouraging, but there is no new category or volume trend overall that supports an expansive growth phase in 2026.

Margins have improved in the past few quarters. Operating Margins were around 26% in Q1 FY2026, supported by supply chain and cost rationalizations, more than operating leverage. On the supply chain side, Supply Chain 3.0 and digital tools are supporting efficiencies. There are plans around headcount and product streamlining (Fem Care business, Pakistan business model changes) to drive further cost optimizations. Over the next two years, PG intends to reduce ~15% of its non-manufacturing workforce.

Since the margin improvements are on the cost and efficiency side, I see these gains to be partially offset by competitive pressures, tariffs and promotional intensity at some point. Any innovation-led boost to margins could change that outlook, but currently the view on margins remain positive but affecting only short-term expansion.

Price and Yields

There is no pressure on the yield story. The payout ratio shows sufficient room to maintain a 69 year growth streak, close to 2.9% at current share prices. Since the yield looks relatively stable and even growing at over 6% over the past 5 years, even an income focused Buy thesis hinges on the outlook for share prices.

I ran a DCF model with a 3% revenue growth projected for the next 5 years. This is reasonably aggressive given the volume stagnation and acknowledges sustained pricing power in the face of significant competition. Growth in China and Latin America is encouraging, but I cannot foresee continued growth signs there to initiate a material new leg of topline growth overall. China growth, in particular, could be volatile and may need continued distribution, innovation and go-to-market interventions. All in all, unless sustained trends arise, a 3% growth appears more than reasonable despite the macro backdrop in North America and Europe.

My DCF model also assumes a continued FCF margin in the higher teens (18%) - the higher end of the FCF margin trends seen in the past decade. This despite margins likely to remain balanced due to competition/promotions, tariffs on one hand and limited runway of optimization on supply chain and product lines on the other.

A terminal growth rate of 2.5% is assumed (only slightly below the higher end of growth assumed for the next 5 years) and a conservative WACC is also assumed at 6.5%. This is what a bull case looks like at the moment. The fair value of this bull case shows a buffer of ~7% from current share prices with all the aggressive margin and growth assumptions working through the macro and competitive headwinds.

A more realistic base case, assuming 2.5% growth, 2% terminal growth rate and 15% FCF margins shows a fair value of closer to $115. Even accounting for a premium valuation for PG's pricing power and brand moat, I find anything below $125 a decent entry point for fresh Buys. Those ranges make the yields more lucrative and open up some upside possibilities in share prices.

A Very Good Hold But not a Buy

Since the current growth and margin expectations are more than built into the current share prices, I don't see scope for a Buy rating on PG, despite it's lower valuations compared to recent history. The thesis for PG will tilt if growth revives through volumes. This could happen due to the return of favorable macro conditions, or pathbreaking innovation or new category foray, which is not evident today. Even margins could receive a boost if the tariff deadlocks are lifted at some point.

Untill then PG's story remains one of pockets of geographical growth offset by subdued performance in North America and Europe. Product mix and pricing plays could keep organic growth alive to some extent, but are not expected to explode beyond the guided ~2% at the midpoint in 2026 and beyond. Margin gains due to optimization are great defensive tactical improvements for the short term, but structural operating leverage needs to return.

I See PG a decent Hold for an income focused portfolio and downside risks could remain limited due to PG's strong performance and brand and pricing power moat. But there is not immediate catalyst for fresh capital deployment. Entries below $125 could see better risk reward asymmetry to exploit.

data.title: Silicon Motion: Gen5, Mobile, And Early Enterprise Traction Strengthen The Buy Case
data.description:
I like the setup that Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (SIMO) has, especially after the Q3 25 earnings report came in. Revenue and margins are up, the Gen5 mix stepped up faster than I expected, and the demand for eMMC/UFS is still very solid. Added to that is enterprise traction, where we’ve seen more wins with MonTitan and AI boot drives. The long-term model is very solid, and my stance is that the stock is trading below what I expect for a controller vendor that is operating at this level of margin and mix.

Silicon Motion’s Q3 2025 Numbers and Q4 Guidance

Since we already have many of the numbers and financials listed above, I’m going to give my commentary on them quickly and move on to the operational side of things.

So, first things first: revenue. It is up 22% sequentially and 14% on a year-over-year basis. Margins (gross and operating) are also up, climbing back to the levels of SIMO’s best years, and that’s largely because of how well the product mix is delivering. I like it.

Operating leverage is also improving, which is expected, and management is so confident in the expansion that it is guiding for 19-20% operating margins in Q4. I don’t know if they’ll get there, but I wouldn’t be surprised, given all the tailwinds and operational progress I’ll discuss later.

Then, we have capital allocation, which is steady too. The company’s balance sheet is clean: cash is ~$272 million, down $10 million from Q2, and that is because of the dividend payments and inventory increases to support its business ramp. SIMO also spent around $20 million in capex during the quarter, but that is barely a fraction of its revenue, and it makes sense because the company is still in a controller-heavy design cycle, not a manufacturing cycle that requires larger investments.

Operational Progress

Let’s start with some of the big leaps SIMO has made since my August article on the company. Client SSD revenue is up over 20% sequentially, and Gen5 is moving faster than I expected. According to management, the company’s SM2508 controller now makes up >15% of client SSD revenue, about 5 percentage points higher than in Q2. And we can expect the ramp-up across SSD to increase when its 4-channel DRAM-less PCIe 5.0 controller lands in Q4.

The overall outlook for this segment is very positive because it is gaining ASP leverage and share at the same time. I wouldn’t be surprised if SIMO’s gross margin comfortably passes the 49.5% mark in Q4 because that margin profile depends on the product mix doing some really heavy lifting.

Then, there’s auto and mobile (eMMC/UFS). This segment grew >20% sequentially too, which is in line with what we expected, going off management’s guidance from earlier in the year. However, I’m more pleased with how well the company is delivering in line with higher demand. The smartphone market is buying more UFS controllers, and there’s broader adoption across industrial and early automotive programs.

As I said in August, eMMC/UFS is the segment that gives SIMO more flexibility to operate without bothering too much about sales cycles because of its steady demand and contributions. Now that it is scaling nicely, we can see the results showing up in the margins too. I also discussed how quickly automotive can scale up to 10% of total revenue and why it could be realistic heading into FY26.

Now, we don’t have hard numbers yet, but the fact that Tesla, Mercedes, Honda, and Geely & BYD (the latter two being big Chinese auto manufacturers that are expanding globally) are ordering more products from SIMO is very good news. Geely and BYD are the ones to watch for me, because they’re taking a larger part of the cheaper-EV market share abroad, and SIMO expanding its relationship with them is crucial if it is going to hit the 10%-of-revenue target in automotive.

Finally, I want to touch on the enterprise segment, which is the foundation of my long-term outlook for the company. MonTitan got another six design wins in Q3, and it is in production qualifying with several Tier-1 NAND makers and hyperscale-related infrastructure customers. Those aren’t small milestones, and they line up nicely with my expectations for the segment.

To be clear, the wins aren’t yet obvious in the numbers, but SIMO is now shipping boot-drive controllers to the biggest AI GPU makers for their DPU products. Besides that, it is also getting into qualification cycles for the next generation of DPUs for these clients, so the runway is pretty extensive. All things considered, SIMO is executing ahead of schedule, and that’s not something I was certain of back in August.

Valuation

Just going off the numbers from Q3 and guidance for Q4, I can make a case for SIMO’s stock still having a lot of room to expand, both in the near- and long term. The company is likely going to wrap up 2025 with an annualized revenue run rate that is close to $900 million. Its gross margin is in the high 4s, and its operating margin is moving back up towards the low 20s.

I’m framing the upside using a DCF model I ran on GuruFocus. I used EPS without non-recurring items, and I assumed a 7% growth rate for a decade with a terminal growth rate of 3%. I believe those are conservative estimates. With those inputs, the stock’s fair value comes out to ~$126/share, and I like it.

Consider this: 7% long-term EPS growth is far below what SIMO’s current margin and mix trajectory can support, and the terminal rate is just about right for a mature semiconductor business. The upside from today’s price of ~$85/share is very promising at nearly 50%, but let us even consider a near-term earnings bridge.

Q3’s numbers and the Q4 guide show us that the company has a very clear path to $4–4.5 in normalized EPS and healthy free cash flow. With that range, a forward P/E multiple of 25x gives us a price range of $100–113/share, which is still a notable increase from the current valuation.

If we look at it from an EV/EBITDA perspective, it’s pretty much the same conclusion. So, I’m confident that SIMO’s value will rise commensurate with what the fundamentals are showing us.

Things To Keep An Eye On

  • A big part of the company’s client SSD scaling depends on the Windows 10 sunset and early AI PC builds. Those cycles will likely pull some demand forward, and it is possible that we see Q1 or Q2 2026 looking softer, even if the trajectory is still fine.

  • The design wins for MonTitan are crucial for us to gauge how well the market is receiving it, but it’d be really helpful to see it contribute to the topline as early as possible. Management says 2026 is the key here, so I’m waiting for the first signs of real monetization by the middle of the year.

  • Management is also watching how the tightness and pricing pressure in NAND can move through HDD, DRAM, and HBM. That’s not something I’m bothered about, but given that we’ve already seen simultaneous shortages across all of them, it can squeeze SIMO’s mix shift, so it is worth watching.

It’s A Stable, Expanding Business

SIMO is executing pretty well against my expectations with its ramps and enterprise growth. The financials are also alright, and none of these things seem to be rewarded by the stock. I could chalk it down to a general market downturn, but that remains to be seen, and I like to evaluate a business on its own merits and make a decision regarding its value first. In this case, SIMO passes my holistic valuation test again, so I’m recommending a Buy.

data.title: Bath & Body Works: With Dividend In Danger, Investors Should Shift To Bonds
data.description:
Introduction

On Thursday, Bath & Body Works (BBWI) shares fell nearly 25% after the retailer posted worse-than-expected earnings. In addition to missing earnings, the company guided a decline in sales for the fourth quarter versus expectations of growth, which fueled the sell-off. Back in April, I discussed how debt and cash secured put options were income opportunities for investors. Now, I believe income investors should shift toward investing in the company’s bonds, specifically the notes maturing in 2035.

Bath & Body Works Earnings

The headwinds affecting Bath & Body Works clearly strengthened in the third quarter. While year-to-date net sales are slightly higher than a year ago, net sales on a quarterly basis were lower than a year ago. To add pressure to gross profit, despite lower sales, the company’s cost of goods was higher than in the same quarter a year ago.

Gross profit remains higher year-to-date than in the same period a year ago, but on a quarterly basis, gross profit fell by 6% to $658 million. Making matters worse, the company’s overhead costs, listed as general administrative, and operating costs, were $15 million higher in the third quarter compared to the same quarter a year earlier and $73 million higher on a year-to-date basis.

With revenue and costs each heading in the wrong direction, operating income was down $57 million on a quarterly basis compared to a year ago at $161 million, and year-to-date operating income is down $61 million at $527 million. Fortunately, operating income is still higher than the company’s interest expense, leading to positive net income, although it is nearly $100 million lower year-to-date than the same period a year ago.

A Stable Balance Sheet

Despite the challenges with sales and profits, Bath & Body Works balance sheet has remained stable throughout the year. Like any retailer, Bath & Body Works has a cyclical buildup of inventory, so while quarter to quarter increases look scary, inventory numbers are in line with where they were a year ago.

The company holds approximately $2 billion in property and operating lease assets. In order to build its inventory, Bath & Body Works has had to draw down its cash, but at $236 million, there’s more than the company had a year ago. While the inventory build is good for near-term cash flow, the prospect of the retailer needing to discount to get products out the door may negatively affect earnings.

On the liability side, the company’s long-term debt has remained stable. Bath & Body Works was even able to pay off current debt without issuing more bonds, a move that has improved interest expense. The built-in accounts payable is something that concerns me. While it is likely a timing issue, the company’s accounts payable is $270 million higher than the same period a year ago and will negatively impact cash flow in the fourth quarter when those bills are paid.

The Cash Flow Statement Holds Why I Downgraded the Shares

While the fourth quarter is the banner quarter for cash flow, trends in the first nine months of the fiscal year can give us some insight into how the company is managing its cash. For Bath & Body Works, operating cash flow grew by almost $300 million in the first nine months of the fiscal year, but that entire variance was due to the growth of accounts payable.

After capital expenditures, free cash flow stood at $51 million for the first nine months of 2025. While free cash flow is positive, and that’s good for bondholders as it demonstrates the ability for the company to service its debt, the current level of free cash flow is not sufficient to cover the company’s $126 million dividend payments.

Along with not having sufficient cash flow to cover its dividends, Bath & Body Works spent $344 million to buy back shares, a practice that doesn’t make any sense considering its current situation. Because of a usually strong fourth quarter, I believe that Bath & Body Works can have its dividend or its share buybacks, but not both.

Debt Detail & Why the 2035 Maturing Bond Is Best

I believe that reducing its dividend or eliminating its share buyback will give Bath & Body Works the cash it needs to deleverage and invest in whatever assets are needed for sales growth. Fortunately, Bath & Body Works debt is manageable between now and 2029 with less than $500 million coming due in each of the years from 2027 through 2029. Deleveraging is a good long-term strategy, but cutting the dividend to get there will not help the company’s share price in the near term.

As for choosing which bond to invest in, investors need to be careful. The 2029 maturing bond looks attractive because its yield is higher, but the bond is trading above par and is callable immediately. Should the bond be called before the next interest payment, investors would experience a loss on their investment. The 2035 bond is not callable, so fixed-income investors do not need to worry about call risk.

Conclusion

The headwinds that Bath & Body Works is experiencing are finally catching up to the company in the form of sales declines. That, combined with the higher cost of goods, is placing hard pressure on the bottom line. Bath & Body Works needs a turnaround plan, and I don’t think deploying cash to buy back shares would be helpful. Despite these challenges, the company’s solvency remains very intact, and income investors can shift away from the dividend and into the 2035 maturing bond to keep collecting their income.

Price: $99.36

Yield to Maturity: 7.17%

data.title: Near-Perfect Portfolio Strategy For Today's Uncertain Market
data.description:
Introduction:

The market is currently taking a pause from its relentless upward momentum. Is it a temporary (pause) or the start of some serious downturn? No one can really predict such things. Until recently, the market had been constantly making fresh new highs. In fact, the S&P500 has made 48 new all-time highs in 2025, and that is on top of 62 new all-time highs in 2024. The bull market that started after the tech crash of 2022 has lifted the S&P500 by nearly 90% from its lows in 2022. However, we should also recognize that much of those gains have been concentrated in 10-15 technology stocks, including most of the magnificent-7 stocks. Nearly 37% of the S&P500 represents the technology sector, which is just one of the 11 sectors. The AI (Artificial Intelligence) hype has lifted the valuations of many companies disproportionately. In fact, some so-called experts have been calling this a “rational” AI bubble. Another anecdote of this rational bubble is that recently, NVIDIA (NVDA), the semiconductor giant, became the first $5 trillion company in the world.

However, beneath the surface, there have been signs of macroeconomic stress. Moreover, the stock market does not necessarily reflect the health of Main Street. It is clear to anyone who wants to look that the consumer is stretched out, especially the low-income consumer. The job market is stagnant if not already declining. Interest rates have been too high for too long, which has impacted the housing market. The housing market is in doldrums, due to high interest rates, which have been too high for too long. The Fed has been late in cutting the interest rates, just as they were late in raising them in 2021-22. The interest rates have come down somewhat, but there is still a lack of clarity on further cuts. There is a serious worry about stagflation. So, the big question that often comes is whether the US economy is heading towards a recession. We hope not, but it is difficult to say one way or the other.

In our opinion, it is a good time to have a serious look at your portfolio and weed out risky and speculative stocks. Also, during a prolonged boom period, our portfolios tend to become bloated with many duplicate or similar positions. So, we should try to consolidate into fewer positions; for example, 30 positions may be enough diversification for most folks.

All that said, we should also try to hedge our portfolio without compromising on growth and certainly without any ongoing costs. We are going to discuss one such strategy with three buckets that we like to call the Near-Perfect Strategy. These three distinct buckets will have their own goals and objectives. By doing this, it not only provides diversification in stocks but also the strategic diversification that should handle corrections and panic situations with higher confidence and lower stress.

What is a Near-Perfect Strategy (NPS) Portfolio?

We introduced this strategy back in 2019. There is simply nothing in the investing world that we can call ‘perfect’, we can only hope to be near-perfect. If we can achieve 80% (or more) of our goals, the overall results should be excellent. This was the mindset while designing the Near-Perfect Investment Strategy. This is akin to buying a house to live in for the long term. When we want to buy a house to live in, we are not looking to sell it the next year. Moreover, we want to make sure that the house has a solid foundation and structure, even if it requires some cosmetic upgrades. We also want to make sure that it is in a safe neighborhood with good schools and other common amenities. Also, it offers good value for the money being invested (not overpriced). Once we have purchased the house, we are not interested in monitoring its day-to-day market value, even though we want it to appreciate in the long term. If the housing market crashes, you are not looking to bail out of the house. You are still saving on rent and enjoying all the perks of the house, as you were prior to the crash. So, you don't bother with its price.

In our mind, we should have a long-term investment mindset. A good investment portfolio is one that is likely to meet a majority of your stated goals. Once invested, we can enjoy the income that it provides, but we are not looking at making a fast buck, nor would we need to bail out if the market were to crash next month.

Goals and Objectives:

Let’s define some goals and expectations from the Near-Perfect Strategy portfolio:-

  • The portfolio should provide roughly 5% to 6% income (depending upon needs) that is consistent and reliable, irrespective of how the stock market is performing.
  • The portfolio should preferably exceed or at least match the growth of the S&P500. On a 10 to 15-year horizon, the strategy should provide a 10% (or higher) annualized growth rate in terms of total returns.
  • The strategy should aim to provide 20-25% lower volatility and drawdowns compared to the broader market, resulting in less stress.

How to decide if the NPS Portfolio is for you?

This portfolio should suit most folks, ranging from retirees, near-term retirees, or anyone who wants to plan for retirement in the next 10-15 years. Anyone who aspires to achieve financial freedom early in life should also find this strategy very attractive. That said, it may not be attractive to very young people who are still in the early stages of their accumulation phase. In fact, we can categorize the potential investors (for the NPS portfolio) into three categories.

  1. Retirees and near-term retirees.
  2. Anyone above the age of 50 who wants to transition from a growth-oriented or index-based portfolio to an income and growth portfolio, but with less volatility and less stress.
  3. Anyone who aspires to achieve financial freedom early in life.

The Near-Perfect Strategy Portfolio:

The goals defined in the previous section are quite ambitious, as this aims to be a near-perfect portfolio. Nonetheless, they are not very hard to achieve with proper strategic planning and standing firm with the strategy in the good times and bad. This requires a strong level of conviction with the chosen strategy. If you do not have a strong conviction, it is best not to start such a strategy. Sure, one size does not fit all, and what may be appropriate for one person may not be so for another. So, it is always best to do your further research and due diligence until you are convinced of the strategy.

This portfolio will have three buckets.

  • DGI bucket with likely 3-4% Income, 10% growth
  • Asset-based buy-and-hold bucket with 5% income, 10% plus growth
  • Hedging bucket with a Rotation strategy aiming to beat the S&P500 handily.

(I) The DGI Bucket: 35% Allocation

We consider the DGI (Dividend Growth Investing) bucket to serve the role of the foundational bucket in the overall portfolio. The income expectations from this bucket are moderate at best, in the range of 4%. This is mostly a buy-and-hold bucket. There are two ways to form this bucket: passive or active. In the case of a passive bucket, we could just invest in five or six funds or ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds), and hold them for a long time. There should be no action required once the portfolio is built carefully in the beginning. The second option requires more active participation from the investor. In this option, we will invest in 15-20 carefully-selected individual stocks. Though this will be a buy-and-hold bucket, it will still require some monitoring and adjustments at least on a semi-annual or annual basis.

In this article, we will focus on the active bucket of individual stocks. One way to form this bucket (without having an extensive knowledge of individual stocks) is to start with the top-10 positions of the 5 most popular dividend funds, giving us 50 stocks. We then weed out the duplicates from this set of 50. This will result in something like 25 names. From this set of 25, we select 15 to 20 stocks based on different industries and on our income goals.

Step-1:

We will take the top-10 positions from each of the five funds:

  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD),
  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield Fund ETF (VYM),
  • iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY),
  • Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV),
  • JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Prem Income ETF (JEPQ).

Step-2: Weed out the duplicates. We will be left with the following 41 stocks.

Step-3: We will remove stocks that pay no dividends. This removes TSLA, NFLX, AMD, and AMZN, leaving us 37 names.

Step-4: At this stage, we have multiple names from each industry segment. We will keep those who have a better dividend record in terms of dividend yield, 5-year growth, and number of years of consistent dividend growth. We remove 16 names: KEY, AAVMY, BAC, KO, ABBV, MRK, PFE, COP, XOM, WMT, NVDA, META, STX, PM, D, and ES. We also remove Ford (F) as it has some risk of cutting dividends. We will now be left with 20 names (37-17), which are presented below.

The selected stocks are (ADM), (JPM), (PEP), (VZ), (PG), (LMT), (V), (AMGN), (UNH), (BMY), (JNJ), (HD), (CVX), (AVGO), (GOOGL), (CSCO), (AAPL), (MSFT), (MO), and (EIX).

Table-1: DGI Bucket

It is apparent that the overall bucket is highly balanced and diversified. The dividend yield is moderate, but if reinvested for a few years, it should cross 4% quickly. Now, we can perform this exercise annually (or once every two years) to rebalance the portfolio.

(III) The Hedging Bucket: 35% Allocation

This is the only bucket in this portfolio that is not a buy-and-hold type. Instead, it will require monthly trading, but mostly one or two trades a month. For this reason, it will be much more preferable to have this bucket inside a tax-deferred type of account. That being said, most likely, it will require less than an hour's worth of work (likely much less) on a monthly basis.

We will have the same set of securities as in Bucket-2, except that we will replace QQQ with ProShares Ultra QQQ ETF (QLD), which is a 2x QQQ fund. Now, for a buy-and-hold portfolio, we do not want a QLD type of security, but this is a Rotation portfolio and will hold QLD only when it meets our criteria.

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of (QQQ) after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside a rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only when it is performing well. More conservative investors could replace it with QQQ.

The process to determine the monthly rotation:

Step-1: At the end of each month, we will calculate the performance (total returns, including any dividends) of all six securities over the last 3 months.

Step-2: We will invest in the top two securities (with 50% each) for the next month; however, with a caveat (see step-3). Alternatively, you could just pick only ONE top-performing asset, which will improve performance but will also increase risk, volatility, and drawdowns.

Step-3: We will see if any of the top two securities have performed worse than the return provided by cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then we will replace that security with cash for the next month (cash can be a money-market fund).

Step-4: We will keep the investment from Step-2 (or Step-3) for the entire month.

Step-5: At the end of the month, we will repeat the process (from Step-1 through 3).

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

In addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth, this rotation strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (our rough estimate at 5%). Most of the securities provide significant income except QLD. Also, due to rotation, at times, the hedging security (cash or money-market fund) with a lower yield may replace the regular security with a higher yield.
